Torii and haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Shizuoka|Q174691|WD=Q174691|1=Shizuoka (city)}}{{ill|lt=Shimizu-ku|Q79385|WD=Q79385|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}}Yamakiri 101 | 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|03|17|N|138|29|10.1|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small) / former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | 例祭={{ill|lt=October 17|Q2962|WD=Q2962|1=October 17}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Kusanagi
Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|35|03|17|N|138|29|10.1|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250|coord={{coord|35|03|17|N|138|29|10.1|E}}}} }} '''Kusanagi Shrine''' (くさなぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Yamakiri, {{ill|lt=Shimizu-ku|Q79385|WD=Q79385|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka|Q174691|WD=Q174691|1=Shizuoka (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. === Summary === The founding era is unknown. Shrine tradition says that when {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} made his eastern campaign, he established a main sanctuary here, and later when {{ill|lt=Kibi Takehiko|Q18226257|WD=Q18226257|1=Kibi no Takehiko}}, vice commander of that campaign, came to rule this area, he built shrine buildings at the former main sanctuary and enshrined Yamato Takeru. It was once also called "Kyūman-hassen-sha" (Ninety-eight-thousand Shrine), said to have enshrined 98,000 people who accompanied Yamato Takeru's eastern campaign, and at rites 98,000 {{ill|lt=heihaku offerings|Q11481885|WD=Q11481885|1=Hanpei}} were dedicated. The oldest record in documents appears in the '{{ill|lt=Suruga no Kuni Fudoki|Q11668491|WD=Q11668491|1=Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}', saying official offerings were presented in year 1 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seimu|Q316672|WD=Q316672|1=Emperor Seimu}} (133). In a variant text of the Ruijū Rikkokushi, it says that in year 1 of {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} (859) it was granted Junior Second Rank in the {{ill|lt=shinkai|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} divine rank system. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' it is listed as "Kusanagi Shrine, Ihara District, Suruga Province," ranked as small shrine. Since {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Shrine|Q11618229|WD=Q11618229|1=Kusanagi Shrine}} in Udo District (present Shimizu-ku Kusanagi), also a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, has the same name and also enshrines Yamato Takeru, this shrine was called "East Kusanagi Shrine" until the Edo period to distinguish them. In Meiji 6 it became a {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}, returning to the original name Kusanagi Shrine. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} (main deity) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ototachibana-hime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Kibi Takehiko|Q18226257|WD=Q18226257|lt=Kibi Takehiko-no-Mikoto|1=Kibi no Takehiko}} * Ōtomo no Takehi no Muraji no Mikoto * Kashiwade no Nanatsukumu-sune no Mikoto * {{ill|lt=Wakatakehiko-no-Mikoto|Q11596350|WD=Q11596350|1=Wakatakehiko}} * Misuki-tomo-mimi-takehiko no Mikoto === Sessha and massha === * Tsushima Shrine * Tenman-gū * Shirahige Shrine * Inō Shrine * Kotohira Shrine === Location === * Yamakiri 101, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka, Shizuoka Prefecture === Nearby facilities === * {{ill|lt=Shimizu National Training Center|Q11560750|WD=Q11560750|1=Shimizu National Training Center}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=Miikehira Kofun|Q107504282|WD=Q107504282|1=Miikedaira-kofun}} * {{ill|lt=Toka Shrine (Shizuoka)|Q119929612|WD=Q119929612|lt=Toka Shrine|1=Toga Shrine}} * {{ill|lt=Toyoyuki Shrine|Q119929613|WD=Q119929613|1=Toyoyuki Shrine}} === Bibliography === * "Kusanagi Shrine" in 'Ihara-gun Ihara Village Gazetteer,' Taishō 2 === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === * Torii and haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Shizuoka|Q174691|WD=Q174691|1=Shizuoka (city)}}{{ill|lt=Shimizu-ku|Q79385|WD=Q79385|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}}Yamakiri 101 | 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|03|17|N|138|29|10.1|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small) / former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | 例祭={{ill|lt=October 17|Q2962|WD=Q2962|1=October 17}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Kusanagi
Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|35|03|17|N|138|29|10.1|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250|coord={{coord|35|03|17|N|138|29|10.1|E}}}} }} '''Kusanagi Shrine''' (くさなぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Yamakiri, {{ill|lt=Shimizu-ku|Q79385|WD=Q79385|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka|Q174691|WD=Q174691|1=Shizuoka (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. === Summary === The founding era is unknown. Shrine tradition says that when {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} made his eastern campaign, he established a main sanctuary here, and later when {{ill|lt=Kibi Takehiko|Q18226257|WD=Q18226257|1=Kibi no Takehiko}}, vice commander of that campaign, came to rule this area, he built shrine buildings at the former main sanctuary and enshrined Yamato Takeru. It was once also called "Kyūman-hassen-sha" (Ninety-eight-thousand Shrine), said to have enshrined 98,000 people who accompanied Yamato Takeru's eastern campaign, and at rites 98,000 {{ill|lt=heihaku offerings|Q11481885|WD=Q11481885|1=Hanpei}} were dedicated. The oldest record in documents appears in the '{{ill|lt=Suruga no Kuni Fudoki|Q11668491|WD=Q11668491|1=Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}', saying official offerings were presented in year 1 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seimu|Q316672|WD=Q316672|1=Emperor Seimu}} (133). In a variant text of the Ruijū Rikkokushi, it says that in year 1 of {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} (859) it was granted Junior Second Rank in the {{ill|lt=shinkai|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} divine rank system. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' it is listed as "Kusanagi Shrine, Ihara District, Suruga Province," ranked as small shrine. Since {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Shrine|Q11618229|WD=Q11618229|1=Kusanagi Shrine}} in Udo District (present Shimizu-ku Kusanagi), also a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, has the same name and also enshrines Yamato Takeru, this shrine was called "East Kusanagi Shrine" until the Edo period to distinguish them. In Meiji 6 it became a {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}, returning to the original name Kusanagi Shrine. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} (main deity) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ototachibana-hime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Kibi Takehiko|Q18226257|WD=Q18226257|lt=Kibi Torii 1.jpg|250px]]
Torii and haiden | 所在地={{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Shizuoka|Q174691|WD=Q174691|1=Shizuoka (city)}}{{ill|lt=Shimizu-ku|Q79385|WD=Q79385|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}}Yamakiri 101 | 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|35|03|17|N|138|29|10.1|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 祭神={{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} | 社格={{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} (small) / former {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}} | 例祭={{ill|lt=October 17|Q2962|WD=Q2962|1=October 17}} | 地図= {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Kusanagi
Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|35|03|17|N|138|29|10.1|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250|coord={{coord|35|03|17|N|138|29|10.1|E}}}} }} '''Kusanagi Shrine''' (くさなぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Yamakiri, {{ill|lt=Shimizu-ku|Q79385|WD=Q79385|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka|Q174691|WD=Q174691|1=Shizuoka (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|WD=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. === Summary === The founding era is unknown. Shrine tradition says that when {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} made his eastern campaign, he established a main sanctuary here, and later when {{ill|lt=Kibi Takehiko|Q18226257|WD=Q18226257|1=Kibi no Takehiko}}, vice commander of that campaign, came to rule this area, he built shrine buildings at the former main sanctuary and enshrined Yamato Takeru. It was once also called "Kyūman-hassen-sha" (Ninety-eight-thousand Shrine), said to have enshrined 98,000 people who accompanied Yamato Takeru's eastern campaign, and at rites 98,000 {{ill|lt=heihaku offerings|Q11481885|WD=Q11481885|1=Hanpei}} were dedicated. The oldest record in documents appears in the '{{ill|lt=Suruga no Kuni Fudoki|Q11668491|WD=Q11668491|1=Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}', saying official offerings were presented in year 1 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seimu|Q316672|WD=Q316672|1=Emperor Seimu}} (133). In a variant text of the Ruijū Rikkokushi, it says that in year 1 of {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|WD=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} (859) it was granted Junior Second Rank in the {{ill|lt=shinkai|Q11591025|WD=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} divine rank system. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' it is listed as "Kusanagi Shrine, Ihara District, Suruga Province," ranked as small shrine. Since {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Shrine|Q11618229|WD=Q11618229|1=Kusanagi Shrine}} in Udo District (present Shimizu-ku Kusanagi), also a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, has the same name and also enshrines Yamato Takeru, this shrine was called "East Kusanagi Shrine" until the Edo period to distinguish them. In Meiji 6 it became a {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}, returning to the original name Kusanagi Shrine. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|WD=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} (main deity) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ototachibana-hime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Kibi Takehiko|Q18226257|WD=Q18226257|lt=Kibi Takehiko-no-Mikoto|1=Kibi no Takehiko}} * Ōtomo no Takehi no Muraji no Mikoto * Kashiwade no Nanatsukumu-sune no Mikoto * {{ill|lt=Wakatakehiko-no-Mikoto|Q11596350|WD=Q11596350|1=Wakatakehiko}} * Misuki-tomo-mimi-takehiko no Mikoto === Sessha and massha === * Tsushima Shrine * Tenman-gū * Shirahige Shrine * Inō Shrine * Kotohira Shrine === Location === * Yamakiri 101, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka, Shizuoka Prefecture === Nearby facilities === * {{ill|lt=Shimizu National Training Center|Q11560750|WD=Q11560750|1=Shimizu National Training Center}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=Miikehira Kofun|Q107504282|WD=Q107504282|1=Miikedaira-kofun}} * {{ill|lt=Toka Shrine (Shizuoka)|Q119929612|WD=Q119929612|lt=Toka Shrine|1=Toga Shrine}} * {{ill|lt=Toyoyuki Shrine|Q119929613|WD=Q119929613|1=Toyoyuki Shrine}} === Bibliography === * "Kusanagi Shrine" in 'Ihara-gun Ihara Village Gazetteer,' Taishō 2 === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4405027 Kusanagi Shrine] (Shizuoka Prefectural Shrine Association) {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kusanagi Shrine (Q11368944)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kusanagi Shrine (Q11368944)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kusanagi Shrine (Q11368944)]][[Category:Shikinai Hiteisha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suruga Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suruga Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:清水区の歴史]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:清水区の建築物]]

Since {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Shrine|Q11618229|WD=Q11618229|1=Kusanagi Shrine}} in Udo District (present Shimizu-ku Kusanagi), also a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}}, has the same name and also enshrines Yamato Takeru, this shrine was called "East Kusanagi Shrine" until the Edo period to distinguish them. Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|35|03|17|N|138|29|10.1|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250|coord={{coord|35|03|17|N|138|29|10.1|E}}}} }} '''Kusanagi Shrine''' (くさなぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Yamakiri, {{ill|lt=Shimizu-ku|Q79385|qid=Q79385|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka|Q174691|qid=Q174691|1=Shizuoka (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|qid=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|qid=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. === Summary === The founding era is unknown. Shrine tradition says that when {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|qid=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} made his eastern campaign, he established a main sanctuary here, and later when {{ill|lt=Kibi Takehiko|Q18226257|qid=Q18226257|1=Kibi no Takehiko}}, vice commander of that campaign, came to rule this area, he built shrine buildings at the former main sanctuary and enshrined Yamato Takeru. It was once also called "Kyūman-hassen-sha" (Ninety-eight-thousand Shrine), said to have enshrined 98,000 people who accompanied Yamato Takeru's eastern campaign, and at rites 98,000 {{ill|lt=heihaku offerings|Q11481885|qid=Q11481885|1=Hanpei}} were dedicated. The oldest record in documents appears in the '{{ill|lt=Suruga no Kuni Fudoki|Q11668491|qid=Q11668491|1=Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}', saying official offerings were presented in year 1 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seimu|Q316672|qid=Q316672|1=Emperor Seimu}} (133). In a variant text of the Ruijū Rikkokushi, it says that in year 1 of {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|qid=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} (859) it was granted Junior Second Rank in the {{ill|lt=shinkai|Q11591025|qid=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} divine rank system. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' it is listed as "Kusanagi Shrine, Ihara District, Suruga Province," ranked as small shrine. Since {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Shrine|Q11618229|qid=Q11618229|1=Kusanagi Shrine}} in Udo District (present Shimizu-ku Kusanagi), also a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}}, has the same name and also enshrines Yamato Takeru, this shrine was called "East Kusanagi Shrine" until the Edo period to distinguish them. In Meiji 6 it became a {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}, returning to the original name Kusanagi Shrine. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|qid=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} (main deity) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ototachibana-hime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Kibi Takehiko|Q18226257|qid=Q18226257|lt=Kibi Takehiko-no-Mikoto|1=Kibi no Takehiko}} * Ōtomo no Takehi no Muraji no Mikoto * Kashiwade no Nanatsukumu-sune no Mikoto * {{ill|lt=Wakatakehiko-no-Mikoto|Q11596350|qid=Q11596350|1=Wakatakehiko}} * Misuki-tomo-mimi-takehiko no Mikoto === Sessha and massha === * Tsushima Shrine * Tenman-gū * Shirahige Shrine * Inō Shrine * Kotohira Shrine === Location === * Yamakiri 101, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka, Shizuoka Prefecture === Nearby facilities === * {{ill|lt=Shimizu National Training Center|Q11560750|qid=Q11560750|1=Shimizu National Training Center}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=Miikehira Kofun|Q107504282|qid=Q107504282|1=Miikedaira-kofun}} * {{ill|lt=Toka Shrine (Shizuoka)|Q119929612|qid=Q119929612|lt=Toka Shrine|1=Toga Shrine}} * {{ill|lt=Toyoyuki Shrine|Q119929613|qid=Q119929613|1=Toyoyuki Shrine}} === Bibliography === * "Kusanagi Shrine" in 'Ihara-gun Ihara Village Gazetteer,' Taishō 2 === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4405027 Kusanagi Shrine] (Shizuoka Prefectural Shrine Association) {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kusanagi Shrine (Q11368944)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kusanagi Shrine (Q11368944)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kusanagi Shrine (Q11368944)]][[Category:Shikinaisha in Suruga Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suruga Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:清水区の歴史]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:清水区の建築物]]

Shrine|coordinates= {{coord2|35|03|17|N|138|29|10.1|E}}|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250|coord={{coord|35|03|17|N|138|29|10.1|E}}}} }} '''Kusanagi Shrine''' (くさなぎじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Yamakiri, {{ill|lt=Shimizu-ku|Q79385|qid=Q79385|1=Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka|Q174691|qid=Q174691|1=Shizuoka (city)}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|qid=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}} and its former {{ill|lt=shrine rank|Q10444029|qid=Q10444029|1=Shakaku}} was {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}. === Summary === The founding era is unknown. Shrine tradition says that when {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|qid=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} made his eastern campaign, he established a main sanctuary here, and later when {{ill|lt=Kibi Takehiko|Q18226257|qid=Q18226257|1=Kibi no Takehiko}}, vice commander of that campaign, came to rule this area, he built shrine buildings at the former main sanctuary and enshrined Yamato Takeru. It was once also called "Kyūman-hassen-sha" (Ninety-eight-thousand Shrine), said to have enshrined 98,000 people who accompanied Yamato Takeru's eastern campaign, and at rites 98,000 {{ill|lt=heihaku offerings|Q11481885|qid=Q11481885|1=Hanpei}} were dedicated. The oldest record in documents appears in the '{{ill|lt=Suruga no Kuni Fudoki|Q11668491|qid=Q11668491|1=Suruga no Kuni Fudoki}}', saying official offerings were presented in year 1 of {{ill|lt=Emperor Seimu|Q316672|qid=Q316672|1=Emperor Seimu}} (133). In a variant text of the Ruijū Rikkokushi, it says that in year 1 of {{ill|lt=Jōgan (Japan)|Q1075635|qid=Q1075635|lt=Jōgan|1=Jōgan}} (859) it was granted Junior Second Rank in the {{ill|lt=shinkai|Q11591025|qid=Q11591025|1=Shinkai (divine rank)}} divine rank system. In the '{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}' it is listed as "Kusanagi Shrine, Ihara District, Suruga Province," ranked as small shrine. Since {{ill|lt=Kusanagi Shrine|Q11618229|qid=Q11618229|1=Kusanagi Shrine}} in Udo District (present Shimizu-ku Kusanagi), also a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|qid=Q134917286}}, has the same name and also enshrines Yamato Takeru, this shrine was called "East Kusanagi Shrine" until the Edo period to distinguish them. In Meiji 6 it became a {{ill|lt=gōsha|Q134917316|qid=Q134917316|1=Gō-sha}}, returning to the original name Kusanagi Shrine. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|lt=Yamato Takeru|Q461258|qid=Q461258|lt=Yamato Takeru-no-Mikoto|1=Yamato Takeru}} (main deity) * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ototachibana-hime-no-Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} * {{ill|lt=Kibi Takehiko|Q18226257|qid=Q18226257|lt=Kibi Takehiko-no-Mikoto|1=Kibi no Takehiko}} * Ōtomo no Takehi no Muraji no Mikoto * Kashiwade no Nanatsukumu-sune no Mikoto * {{ill|lt=Wakatakehiko-no-Mikoto|Q11596350|qid=Q11596350|1=Wakatakehiko}} * Misuki-tomo-mimi-takehiko no Mikoto === Sessha and massha === * Tsushima Shrine * Tenman-gū * Shirahige Shrine * Inō Shrine * Kotohira Shrine === Location === * Yamakiri 101, Shimizu-ku, Shizuoka, Shizuoka Prefecture === Nearby facilities === * {{ill|lt=Shimizu National Training Center|Q11560750|qid=Q11560750|1=Shimizu National Training Center}} === See also === * {{ill|lt=Miikehira Kofun|Q107504282|qid=Q107504282|1=Miikedaira-kofun}} * {{ill|lt=Toka Shrine (Shizuoka)|Q119929612|qid=Q119929612|lt=Toka Shrine|1=Toga Shrine}} * {{ill|lt=Toyoyuki Shrine|Q119929613|qid=Q119929613|1=Toyoyuki Shrine}} === Bibliography === * "Kusanagi Shrine" in 'Ihara-gun Ihara Village Gazetteer,' Taishō 2 === Notes === {{Reflist}} === External links === * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4405027 Kusanagi Shrine] (Shizuoka Prefectural Shrine Association) {{神道 横}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kusanagi Shrine (Q11368944)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kusanagi Shrine (Q11368944)]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kusanagi Shrine (Q11368944)]][[Category:Shikinaisha in Suruga Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Suruga Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:清水区の歴史]] [[Category:Yamato Takeru Legend]] [[Category:清水区の建築物]]
